name: Kaustubha Gem
description: The Kaustubha is a divine gem, considered one of the most precious jewels in Hindu mythology. It emerged during the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the cosmic ocean by the gods (Devas) and demons (Asuras) to obtain the Amrita, the elixir of immortality. As the ocean was churned, many wondrous treasures surfaced, and among them was the radiant Kaustubha. This gem is believed to possess immense power and divine energy, symbolizing purity, prosperity, and radiance. Vishnu, the preserver of the universe, chose the Kaustubha to adorn his chest, signifying its supreme importance and his role as the protector of cosmic order. The gem is often depicted as a large, brilliant jewel, radiating light and representing the divine essence of Vishnu. Its presence on Vishnu's chest serves as a constant reminder of the divine's accessibility and its commitment to upholding dharma. The Kaustubha is not merely an ornament; it is a symbol of Vishnu's divine attributes, his compassion, and his unwavering dedication to the well-being of the universe. Legends say that the gem's radiance can dispel darkness and negativity, bringing peace and prosperity to those who behold it. It is a potent symbol of hope and divine grace, a testament to the power of good over evil.
subtitle: Gem of Vishnu; Divine Radiance
origin: Churning of the Cosmic Ocean (Samudra Manthana)

keyMyths.title: Samudra Manthana
keyMyths.description: The Kaustubha gem emerged during the churning of the cosmic ocean, a collaborative effort between gods and demons to obtain Amrita. It was one of the many treasures that surfaced during this epic event.
keyMyths.source: Various Puranas, including the Vishnu Purana and Bhagavata Purana

keyMyths.title: Vishnu's Adornment
keyMyths.description: Vishnu chose the Kaustubha to wear on his chest, symbolizing its divine significance and his role as the preserver. This signifies the gem's supreme value and its connection to Vishnu's divine essence.
keyMyths.source: Various Puranas, including the Vishnu Purana and Bhagavata Purana
